metadata.seed: Well, just kind of. Helpless and hopeless, really. Like I said, just finding a job. I really don't know what I want to do anymore. After I graduated, I thought I was gonna get into business, but I just really don't know if that's for me. My parents always kind of gave me everything too. So the reason why I have my own apartment is because they bought it for me. But it was under the conditions of having to be in the same town as them. And so it's like I'm at a loss and I could continue just loving off my parents money, but then that kind of feels. I feel like I don't have a purpose. Like my purpose really just going to be to live off my parents money and kind of do whatever they want me to do and just to keep their money and their resources. So I'm just kind of at a loss right now in my life.

patient.query: Well, just kind of. Helpless and hopeless, really. Like I said, just finding a job. I really don't know what I want to do anymore. So the reason why I have my own apartment is because they bought it for me. But it was under the conditions of having to be in the same town as them. And so it's like I'm at a loss and I could continue just loving off my parents money, but then that kind of feels. I feel like I don't have a purpose. Like my purpose really just going to be to live off my parents money and kind of do whatever they want me to do and just to keep their money and their resources. So I'm just kind of at a loss right now in my life.

triggers: living in the same town as parents
automatic_thoughts: I don't know what I want to do anymore
business may not be for me
I feel like I don't have a purpose
my purpose is to live off my parents' money

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling stuck and uncertain about your next steps. What are some things that have made you feel hopeful or motivated in the past, even if they seemed small? How might you explore those possibilities without feeling pressured to have everything figured out right now?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I do feel a bit stuck. I remember times when I felt really inspired, like when I took that course last year. Just learning something new felt refreshing. But there’s this pressure to decide what I want to do next, and it kind of just freezes me up. I want to explore things, but I keep doubting if I'm even capable of finding anything that excites me.

triggers: pressure to decide what to do next
automatic_thoughts: I keep doubting if I'm even capable
nothing will excite me

llm_response.response: It sounds like the pressure to decide what to do next is making you feel stuck and doubting your ability to find something exciting. What evidence do you have that you’re not capable of discovering something that sparks your interest? Can you recall a time when you did feel inspired or excited by something new?

patient.query: I feel really stuck, to be honest. There were moments when I felt inspired, like when I took that course last year—it was refreshing. But now I feel this pressure to figure out my next steps, and it just freezes me up. I want to explore options, but I keep doubting if I'll find anything that excites me.

triggers: pressure to figure out next steps
automatic_thoughts: I feel stuck
I doubt I'll find anything that excites me

llm_response.response: It sounds like the pressure to decide is making you feel stuck and frozen. What evidence do you have that nothing will spark your interest? Have there been moments in the past—like that course you took—when something reignited your excitement? How might you test a new option to see if it resonates with you?

patient.query: I just feel really stuck right now. There were moments when I felt inspired, like when I took that course last year; it was refreshing. But now, there's so much pressure to figure out what to do next, and it just freezes me up. I want to explore, but I keep doubting if I'll find anything that actually excites me.

triggers: pressure to figure out what to do next

I keep doubting if I'll find anything that excites me

llm_response.response: It sounds like the pressure to decide what comes next is weighing heavily on you. What kinds of activities or topics have sparked even a small spark of interest in the past, and how might you revisit those? How could you experiment with a few low‑stakes options to see if any of them bring back that sense of excitement you remember from the course?

patient.query: Yeah, the pressure is definitely overwhelming. I remember enjoying a few topics, like creative writing and graphic design; they felt freeing. But trying to revisit them feels daunting, like I have to be good at them right away. I guess I could try some low-stakes things, but I worry that if I don't instantly feel that spark again, I'll just feel worse about it. It’s a bit of a tug-of-war in my head.

automatic_thoughts: I have to be good at them right away
if I don't instantly feel that spark again, I'll just feel worse

llm_response.response: It can feel like a lot of pressure to feel that spark right away, but many people find that enjoyment builds over time. You might try setting small, manageable goals for each session, so the focus shifts from instant excitement to steady progress. Even if the spark isn’t immediate, the act of creating can still bring a sense of accomplishment.

patient.query: I get what you're saying, and I do want to feel that sense of accomplishment. But it's hard not to compare myself to others who seem to find that spark right away. Sometimes, it feels like if I don't feel it instantly, maybe I'm just not cut out for it. I guess I’m just scared that if I try something and it doesn’t go well, it'll just reinforce that belief. It's all a bit overwhelming.

automatic_thoughts: I'm not cut out for it
if I try and fail, it reinforces my belief

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling a lot of pressure when you see others moving forward so quickly. What evidence do you have that you’re not capable of finding that spark, and how might you interpret the situation if you considered that everyone’s path is different? How could you view a setback as a learning step rather than a confirmation of a fixed belief?

patient.query: I don’t know, I guess I just feel like everyone else has it figured out, and I’m just... here. It’s like there’s a timeline that I’m supposed to be following, and I’m behind. I want to believe that everyone's journey is different, but it just makes me feel more lost. The idea of a setback being a learning moment sounds nice, but I can’t help but feel it means I'm stuck in a pattern of not measuring up. What if I'm just not

automatic_thoughts: everyone else has it figured out
I'm behind
everyone's journey is different
I'm stuck in a pattern of not measuring up
what if I'm just not enough

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling lost and anxious when you think about how you stack up against others. What evidence do you have that you’re actually behind? How might you reframe your view of progress to see your own unique path?

patient.query: I guess I just see friends getting promotions or starting projects, and it feels like they’re moving forward while I’m standing still. I know everyone’s journey is different, but it’s hard to shake that feeling of falling behind. Maybe I could reframe things, but right now, it just feels like I’m stuck in this comparison game. It’s exhausting. What if I don’t have anything unique to bring to the table? That thought lingers a lot.

automatic_thoughts: they're moving forward while I'm standing still
I'm falling behind
I'm stuck in this comparison game
I don't have anything unique to bring to the table

llm_response.response: I hear how exhausting it feels to keep seeing others move forward while you feel stuck. What evidence do you have that you’re actually falling behind, and what evidence might suggest you’re making progress in your own way? How could you describe a unique contribution you’re already bringing to your circle, even if it’s not a promotion or a new project?

patient.query: I mean, there are definitely times when I’ve helped friends with their projects, or even just supported them when they were feeling down. I guess that's something, but it feels small compared to a promotion or a big achievement. It’s like I can't shake this feeling that I should be doing more to prove myself. So, even when I think about those moments, they seem overshadowed by what I haven’t accomplished yet. It’s frustrating because I know I have something to offer, but I just

automatic_thoughts: I should be doing more to prove myself
my contributions feel small
my achievements overshadow my efforts

llm_response.response: I hear how frustrated you feel when you compare your supportive moments to bigger milestones. It might help to pause and ask: what evidence do you have that those acts of support are “small” or that they don’t count toward proving yourself? Even small contributions can build trust and open doors—consider how they might add value in ways you haven’t yet seen.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I get that those moments matter, but it still feels like they’re overshadowed by the "big wins." Sometimes I worry that the support I give isn't enough to show what I'm capable of. It’s hard to see it as anything more than just… well, something I should be doing anyway. I want to feel proud of those times, but I constantly find myself measuring them against what society seems to value more, like promotions or accolades. It’s frustrating. I wish I

automatic_thoughts: support isn't enough
should be doing this anyway
society values promotions more

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re worried that these smaller moments don’t measure up to bigger achievements. What evidence do you have that these moments don’t reflect your abilities or worth? How might you reframe them as meaningful in their own right, rather than as something you “should” be doing to prove yourself?
